exemplar.title: How does the involvement of emotion influence the decision-making strategy used in 12th graders?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the involvement of emotion influence the decision-making strategy used in 12th graders?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, well-explained investigation aim and relevance to adolescent decision-making contexts
Comprehensive description of Adaptive Decision Maker Framework with explicit links to the study design
Null and research hypotheses state and operationalize IV and DV effectively
weaknesses: Transition paragraphs are repetitive and could be more concise
Luce et al. (1997) section ends abruptly, lacking experimental detail
Hypothesis could specify coding rubric or reliability checks to fully operationalize measures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Introduction strands meet the 5-6 band descriptors, demonstrating clear relevance, theory linkage, and operationalization. Minor issuesârepetition, incomplete theoretical detail, and missing methodological specificsâprevent a perfect score, so 5/6 reflects the strong but slightly imperfect execution.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but justified to avoid order and conformity effects
Convenience sampling choice is explained with participant demographics and ethical procedures
Materials and instructions are standardized (scripted, native language) to control extraneous variables
weaknesses: Choice of specific materials (scripts, scenario sheets) lacks explanation beyond description
No description of randomization procedure for selecting participants
Sample size justification (power analysis or effect size) is absent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of three Exploration strands reach the explanatory band (3-4), but the variables/materials strand remains descriptive only. The design and sampling are well explained, but missing rationales for materials and randomization, so an overall 3/4 reflects solid yet incomplete exploration planning.

strengths: Descriptive (means, SDs) and inferential (MannâWhitney U with normality tests) statistics are appropriately and accurately applied
Findings are clearly linked to the hypothesis, with correct interpretation of group differences
Statistical table is well organized, showing group means and variability
weaknesses: Graphical presentation contains errors (mislabelled y-axis, missing error bars)
No effect size is reported, limiting understanding of result magnitude
Descriptive table lacks confidence intervals to illustrate estimate precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis fully meets the top-band standard, but graphical and reporting omissions keep this criterion in the 3-4 band. Accurate application of tests and interpretation justify a mid-band score of 4/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with clear reference to the Adaptive Decision Maker Framework and prior literature
Strengths and limitations are relevant and their impacts on validity are explained
Proposed modifications are linked to identified limitations and justified conceptually
weaknesses: Modifications lack detailed implementation plans or prioritization by feasibility
Proposals (e.g., mixed methods, VR stimuli) are not sufficiently concrete
Conclusion reiterates points without ranking future research directions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands demonstrate strong alignment with theory and relevance, and limitations/modifications are addressed. However, lack of concrete detail and prioritization in proposed changes keeps performance at the lower half of the top band. A score of 3/6 acknowledges the thoughtful discussion while noting the need for greater elaboration.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar graph clearly compares group means and aligns with the hypothesis. Axes are labelled and the graph type is suitable. Minor flaws (no error bars, y-axis mislabelled as âGroupsâ) represent small errors but still place the work in the 3-4 band where the graph âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errors.â

The student states statistical results and **links them to the hypothesis**, explicitly rejecting H0 and noting directionality. They further interpret what the shift means for cognitive processing. Interpretation is sound but concise; slight lack of detail on effect size keeps it to 5 rather than 6.

Both null and research hypotheses are written and **contain correctly identified, fully operationalised IV (presence vs absence of explicit high-stakes information) and DV (decision strategy coded as attribute- or alternative-based).** The operationalisation is concrete (instructional wording, binary coding of strategies), satisfying the highest-band requirement.

Convenience sampling is described and the **choice of 12th-graders is explained** in terms of developmental relevance. Participant characteristics (age range, shared curriculum) are provided and ethical procedures are outlined. This goes beyond description, meeting the âexplainedâ standard for band 3-4.

The Adaptive Decision Maker Framework is **accurately described** (limited capacity, strategy switching, attribute- vs. alternative-based processing) and the student **explicitly links** these ideas to the planned manipulation of emotional involvement and the expected strategic shift. Additional theoretical support (Cognitive Load Theory, Somatic Marker Hypothesis) further clarifies the link. This meets the requirement for a full description with a clear connection to the investigation.

Findings are **discussed** in depth against the Adaptive Decision Maker Framework and prior literature, explaining how results support theoretical claims. The discussion is thorough but could integrate counter-evidence; nonetheless it fits the 5-6 band.

The student clearly states the investigationâs aim â to examine whether task-related negative emotion shifts 12th-graders toward attribute-based decision strategies â and then **explains its relevance** by linking adolescent developmental context, high-stakes choices and the need to understand cognitiveâemotional interactions in this age group. The explanation goes beyond a simple identification, showing why the study matters for educational and clinical interventions. This fulfils the descriptor for the top band.

Multiple **strengths and limitations are explained** (population, ecological, construct validity, individual differences) with direct relevance to the investigation. Explanations show how each issue affects interpretation, matching the top band.

The IV/DV and materials (scripts, language choice, scenario sheets) are **described** and some controls (standardised instructions, Turkish language) are mentioned. However, the student does not explicitly justify why these particular controls or materials were chosen over alternatives, nor are all potential confounds addressed. Hence the work fits the descriptive 1-2 band rather than the explanatory 3-4 band.

Proposed modifications (broader sampling, physiological/self-report emotional measures, immersive simulations, mixed methods) are **explicitly linked** to the corresponding limitations and justified. While well argued, they are not extensively elaborated, meriting 5 within the highest band.

The student not only describes the independent-measures experiment but **explains why** it was chosen (avoid carry-over, conformity). They also justify time limits and separation of groups. This explanatory depth aligns with the 3-4 band; minor omissions (no mention of randomisation procedure details or blinding) prevent a seamless top-band award.

Appropriate descriptive (means, SDs) **and** inferential statistics (Mann-Whitney U owing to violated normality; Shapiro-Wilk and Levene tests reported) are used **accurately.** Calculations, p-value and decision are correct and fully interpreted. This satisfies the highest-band descriptor.

annotations.comment: The transition paragraph is overly repetitive, restating prior points about strategy switching. Condense overlapping sentences to improve clarity and brevity.
The operationalisation of IV and DV within the description of the scholarship scenario is clear and concrete, facilitating reproducibility of the manipulation.
In the hypothesis statement, specify how decision strategies will be coded (e.g., coding rubric, inter-rater reliability) to demonstrate full operationalisation.
Sampling description thoroughly details participant demographics, consent procedures, and rationale for convenience sampling within the school context.
The sampling section lacks detail on how participants were randomly selected from the wider 12th-grade cohort. Include the randomization procedure to strengthen internal validity.
The choice to standardise instructions via a scripted aloud reading is well justified to control non-verbal influence on emotional involvement.

The theoretical overview articulates decision-making strategies comprehensively, integrating attribute- and alternative-based approaches. This sets a strong foundation for the investigationâs conceptual framework.
The paragraph describing Luce et al. (1997) ends abruptly, leaving the experimental details unclear. Complete this section by summarizing their manipulation, sample size, and key findings.
Consider explicitly linking FÃ¼lÃ¶pâs definition of decision-making to your chosen measures and scenarios, clarifying how this definition informs your manipulation of emotional involvement.
Justification for modifying the participant population to 12th graders is well articulated, highlighting developmental relevance and unique decision contexts.
The final sentence of the theoretical linkage ends mid-phrase (âaway from deliberateâ¦â), creating a break in flow. Revise to complete this explanation and maintain coherence.
Expand the discussion of Switzer (2009) by explaining precisely how high-stakes decisions differ between adolescents and undergraduates, strengthening the rationale for your sample.
The decision to use Mann-Whitney U following Shapiro-Wilkâs normality violation is appropriate and demonstrates robust inferential reasoning.
Report an effect size (e.g., r) for the Mann-Whitney U result to convey the magnitude of the group difference beyond statistical significance.
Provide justification for your sample size (N=39), including considerations of statistical power or effect size estimates to support your design choice.
Pilot-test the scenario materials to confirm they elicit the intended emotional responses in this age group. Report any pilot findings to bolster construct validity.
The rationale for an independent-measures design to avoid order and conformity effects is well articulated and grounded in relevant literature.
Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) are correctly presented in Table 1, offering a clear overview of group differences in attribute-based decision rates.
Table 1 lacks confidence intervals or graphical error bars, limiting insight into the precision of the group mean estimates. Consider adding these details.
Including 95% confidence intervals for each group mean would strengthen interpretation by quantifying the reliability of observed differences.
The graph depicting mean comparisons correctly addresses the hypothesis but is missing error bars and mislabels the y-axis. This reduces clarity.
Discussion effectively connects findings to the Adaptive Decision Maker Framework and prior literature, reinforcing theoretical implications.
The section on ecological validity outlines limitations well but could propose more concrete examples of multimedia or VR stimuli to enhance realism.
The absence of physiological or validated self-report measures of emotion is acknowledged, highlighting a key construct validity gap.
The strengths section notes native language use and script standardisation, underscoring thoughtful procedural controls.
Proposed mixed-methods modifications lack detail on implementation; specify which qualitative methods and coding frameworks will be used.
The conclusion succinctly integrates findings, limitations, and future directions, reaffirming the studyâs contribution to understanding emotion and strategy use.
The conclusion reiterates several modifications without prioritisation; consider ranking proposals by feasibility or predicted impact to better guide future research.
Incorporate effect size estimates and confidence intervals into the conclusion to quantify the robustness and generalisability of your empirical findings.
